Ordered mode writes data to disk before journaling metadata; writeback mode journals metadata without guaranteeing data order, risking stale data after crash
Writeback mode is always slower than ordered mode
Ordered mode only journals file names

intermediate.question: How does the SCAN (elevator) algorithm work for disk scheduling?
options: It randomly selects the next request to service
The disk arm moves in one direction servicing requests until it reaches the end, then reverses direction and services requests going back
It services only requests on the outermost tracks
It services requests in alphabetical order of file names

intermediate.question: What advantage does C-SCAN have over SCAN?
options: C-SCAN is simpler to implement
C-SCAN provides more uniform wait times by only servicing requests in one direction and returning to the beginning without servicing
C-SCAN uses less disk space
C-SCAN eliminates all seek time

intermediate.question: How does RAID 5 distribute parity information?
options: Parity is stored on a dedicated disk
Parity blocks are distributed across all disks in the array, avoiding the bottleneck of a single parity disk
Parity is not used in RAID 5
Each disk stores its own parity only

intermediate.question: What is wear leveling in SSDs?
options: Evenly distributing weight on the drive housing
Distributing write operations evenly across all flash memory cells to prevent some cells from wearing out faster than others
Reducing the speed of write operations to extend drive life
Leveling the surface of the flash memory chips

intermediate.question: What is the LOOK variant of disk scheduling?
options: The disk arm looks ahead one request at a time
The arm moves in one direction only as far as the last pending request, then reverses, instead of going to the disk's end
It looks at all requests simultaneously before choosing
It skips every other request

intermediate.question: What is the boot block on a disk?
options: A block that prevents booting
The disk block(s) containing the bootstrap loader program that initializes the system and loads the operating system
A block reserved for backup data
The largest block on the disk

intermediate.question: What is disk defragmentation and when is it beneficial?
options: It encrypts disk data for security
It reorganizes scattered file fragments into contiguous blocks, beneficial for HDDs to reduce seek times but unnecessary for SSDs
It deletes duplicate files from the disk
It increases the disk's storage capacity

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of a hot spare disk in a RAID configuration?
options: A disk that stores temporary data
An idle disk that automatically replaces a failed disk in the array, reducing the window of vulnerability
A disk that runs at a higher temperature
A disk used only for read operations

intermediate.question: What is memory-mapped I/O and how does it differ from port-mapped I/O?
options: Memory-mapped I/O is slower than port-mapped I/O in all cases
Memory-mapped I/O maps device registers into the regular memory address space; port-mapped I/O uses separate I/O address space with special instructions
Port-mapped I/O uses regular memory addresses
Memory-mapped I/O requires dedicated I/O instructions

intermediate.question: What is double buffering and why is it used?
options: Using two CPUs for I/O operations
Using two buffers so the device can fill one while the process reads from the other, overlapping I/O and computation
Buffering data twice for error checking
Using buffers of double the normal size

intermediate.question: How does an interrupt vector table work?
options: It stores all device drivers in a single table
It is an array of pointers to interrupt handler routines, indexed by interrupt number, allowing quick dispatch to the correct handler
It converts analog signals to digital interrupts
It counts the number of interrupts per second

intermediate.question: What is the role of the DMA controller during a data transfer?
options: It replaces the CPU entirely during the transfer
It manages the transfer of data between I/O devices and memory, handling addressing, counting, and bus arbitration while the CPU runs other tasks
It only monitors the transfer without participating
It encrypts data during transfer

intermediate.question: How does asynchronous I/O differ from non-blocking I/O?
options: They are identical concepts
Non-blocking I/O returns immediately with available data; asynchronous I/O initiates the operation and notifies the process when it completes, fully decoupling the I/O from the process
Asynchronous I/O blocks the process until completion
Non-blocking I/O uses more memory than asynchronous I/O

intermediate.question: What is the device status table maintained by the OS?
options: A table listing all devices that need repair
A table that tracks the type, address, status, and pending I/O requests for each device in the system
A table of device prices for inventory management
A table mapping device names to driver versions

intermediate.question: What is device-independent I/O software responsible for?
options: Manufacturing hardware devices
Providing uniform interfaces, buffering, error reporting, and device allocation that work regardless of the specific device
Installing device drivers automatically
Converting device signals to audio

intermediate.question: What is an interrupt controller and what does it do?
options: A software program that generates interrupts
Hardware that manages multiple interrupt lines, prioritizes interrupts, and routes them to the CPU
A controller that disables all interrupts permanently
A device that converts interrupts to I/O requests

intermediate.question: What is the access matrix model and how is it used for protection?
options: A matrix that stores encryption keys
A conceptual model with rows for domains (subjects) and columns for objects, where each cell contains the set of operations the domain can perform on the object
A physical matrix of hardware protection circuits
A matrix that maps memory addresses

intermediate.question: How does Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) simplify security management?
options: By eliminating all access control
By assigning permissions to roles instead of individual users, then assigning users to roles, reducing the complexity of managing per-user permissions
By giving all users the same role
By using biometrics exclusively

intermediate.question: What is a buffer overflow attack and why is it dangerous?
options: Filling a printer buffer with too much data
Writing data beyond a buffer's allocated memory to overwrite adjacent memory, potentially hijacking program control flow to execute malicious code
Downloading too many files at once
Overflowing the CPU's instruction cache

intermediate.question: What is sandboxing in operating system security?
options: Running programs on beach-themed desktops
Running a program in an isolated environment with restricted access to system resources, limiting the damage if the program is compromised
Encrypting program data with sand-based algorithms
A technique to speed up program execution

intermediate.question: What is privilege escalation?
options: A user requesting a password reset
An attacker exploiting a vulnerability to gain higher access privileges than initially granted, such as obtaining root access from a regular user account
The process of granting administrator access to new employees
Upgrading the operating system to a newer version

intermediate.question: How does the capability list approach implement the access matrix?
options: It stores one list per object with authorized subjects
Each process holds a list of capabilities (tokens) specifying objects it can access and the permitted operations
It stores a single global list of all permissions
It uses a hash table indexed by user names

intermediate.question: What is a rootkit and why is it particularly dangerous?
options: A toolkit for system administrators to manage root access
Malware that hides its presence by modifying the OS or firmware, making it extremely difficult to detect while maintaining persistent unauthorized access
A set of root vegetables used as a screen saver
A backup tool for the root partition

intermediate.question: What are protection rings and how do they enforce security?
options: Physical rings placed around the CPU for protection
Hierarchical privilege levels (Ring 0 being most privileged) enforced by hardware, where inner rings have more privileges and outer rings are restricted
Software-only protection mechanisms in the firewall
Network security zones for different departments

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of a turnstile synchronization mechanism used in some kernels?
options: To limit the number of threads in the system
A queue structure where blocked threads waiting for a lock are placed, allowing priority inheritance and ordered wakeup
To rotate CPU access among threads
To count the number of active synchronization primitives

intermediate.question: What is the C-LOOK disk scheduling algorithm?
options: A variant of C-SCAN that reverses at the end of the disk
A variant of C-SCAN where the arm only goes as far as the last pending request in each direction before wrapping around, avoiding unnecessary travel to disk edges
A scheduling algorithm that looks at every other request
An algorithm identical to FCFS but circular

intermediate.question: What is the difference between low-level formatting and high-level formatting of a disk?
options: They are the same process with different names
Low-level formatting creates the physical sector structure on the disk surface; high-level formatting creates the file system data structures within a partition
High-level formatting erases the physical platters
Low-level formatting only applies to SSDs

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the I/O request queue in the OS?
options: To store the output of completed I/O operations
To hold pending I/O requests for a device, allowing the scheduler to reorder them for efficiency
To count the total number of I/O operations performed
To buffer all keyboard inputs

intermediate.question: How does caching differ from buffering in the context of I/O?
options: They are identical concepts with different names
A buffer holds the only copy of data during transfer; a cache holds a faster duplicate copy of data that also exists elsewhere
Caching is always slower than buffering
Buffering uses CPU cache while caching uses RAM

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of a digital certificate in security?
options: To compress encrypted data
To bind a public key to an entity's identity, verified by a trusted Certificate Authority, enabling secure key exchange
To digitally sign physical documents
To store all of a user's passwords

intermediate.question: What is a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack?
options: A physical attack on a server's hardware
An attacker secretly intercepts and potentially alters communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other
A type of virus that hides in the middle of a file
An attack that only targets the middle layer of the network stack

intermediate.question: In the context of OS design, what is the key advantage of a microkernel architecture over a monolithic kernel?
options: Better performance from fewer context switches in kernel
Direct hardware access available for all system services
Higher reliability since most services run in user space
Simpler implementation compared to other kernel designs

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of a hypervisor in virtualization?
options: To serve as a real-time antivirus scanning tool
To create and manage virtual machines on hardware
To increase the total RAM capacity of a system
To compile user programs at a much faster speed

intermediate.question: What distinguishes a Type 1 hypervisor from a Type 2 hypervisor?
options: Type 1 runs on top of host OS, Type 2 on hardware
There is no real or meaningful difference between them
Type 1 is inherently slower in performance than Type 2
Type 1 runs directly on hardware, Type 2 on host OS

intermediate.question: In a hard real-time system, what happens when a deadline is missed?
options: The task priority gets increased
The system gradually slows down
The task is automatically retried
The result is a total system failure

intermediate.question: What is the main purpose of the interrupt vector table?
options: To hold addresses of interrupt service routines
To schedule and dispatch CPU tasks to run
To manage the overall file system structure
To permanently store user programs on disk

intermediate.question: What is the concept of 'dual-mode operation' in operating systems and why is it essential?
options: It separates user and kernel modes for protection
It doubles the overall CPU processing speed rate
It enables running two operating systems at once
It allows two users to work at the same time

intermediate.question: In clustered systems, what is the difference between asymmetric clustering and symmetric clustering?
options: Asymmetric has standby node; symmetric all nodes share
Asymmetric uses different OS, symmetric uses same OS
There is no meaningful difference between these approaches
Asymmetric clustering is inherently faster than symmetric

intermediate.question: Which of the following is the main function of an operating system?
options: Compiling source code
Managing hardware resources and providing services to user programs
Designing web pages
Encrypting network traffic
intermediate.explanation: An OS manages CPU, memory, I/O devices, and file systems while providing a convenient interface for applications to use hardware resources.

intermediate.question: Which type of operating system is designed to handle tasks with strict timing constraints?
options: Batch OS
Time-sharing OS
Real-Time OS (RTOS)
Distributed OS
intermediate.explanation: An RTOS guarantees that critical tasks are completed within defined time deadlines, used in systems like medical devices, flight controllers, and industrial automation.

intermediate.question: The kernel is:
options: A user application
The core component of the OS that manages system resources
A type of file system
An input device driver only
intermediate.explanation: The kernel is the central part of the OS. It loads first during booting and remains in memory, managing processes, memory, devices, and system calls.

intermediate.question: A system call provides:
options: A way for users to directly access hardware
An interface between user-level programs and the operating system kernel
A method to install device drivers
A way to boot the computer
intermediate.explanation: System calls are the programmatic interface through which applications request services from the OS kernel, such as file operations, process creation, and memory allocation.

intermediate.question: In a multiprogramming system, the primary goal is to:
options: Run only one program at a time
Maximize CPU utilization by keeping the CPU busy with multiple jobs
Reduce memory usage
Eliminate the need for I/O devices
intermediate.explanation: Multiprogramming keeps multiple jobs in memory so that when one job waits for I/O, the CPU can switch to another, maximizing CPU utilization.

intermediate.question: In a monolithic kernel, all OS services run in:
options: User space
A separate virtual machine
Kernel space as a single large process
Individual containers
intermediate.explanation: A monolithic kernel runs all OS services (file system, drivers, memory management) in kernel space. It is fast but harder to maintain than a microkernel.

intermediate.question: A microkernel OS architecture moves many services to:
options: The hardware layer
User space, keeping only essential functions in the kernel
The BIOS
External servers on the network
intermediate.explanation: A microkernel keeps only the most essential functions (IPC, basic scheduling, memory management) in kernel space. File systems, drivers, and other services run in user space.

intermediate.question: Which of the following is an advantage of a layered OS architecture?
options: Maximum performance due to no abstraction overhead
Modularity and easier debugging since each layer only interacts with adjacent layers
It eliminates the need for a kernel
It does not support virtual memory
intermediate.explanation: Layered architecture organizes the OS into layers where each layer only uses services of the layer below it, making the system modular and easier to debug and verify.

intermediate.question: A hypervisor is used to:
options: Manage file permissions
Create and manage virtual machines by multiplexing hardware
Schedule disk I/O
Compile programs
intermediate.explanation: A hypervisor (Virtual Machine Monitor) allows multiple operating systems to run on a single physical machine by virtualizing hardware resources.

intermediate.question: A process is:
options: A file stored on disk
A program in execution along with its associated resources
A CPU register
A type of memory
intermediate.explanation: A process is an active entity that includes the program code, current activity (program counter), stack, data section, and heap. A program becomes a process when loaded into memory and executed.

intermediate.question: Which data structure stores a process's state information during a context switch?
options: Page table
File Allocation Table
Process Control Block (PCB)
Inode
intermediate.explanation: The PCB contains the process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory management info, scheduling info, and I/O status. It is saved and restored during context switches.

intermediate.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid process state?
options: New
Running
Compiling
Waiting (Blocked)
intermediate.explanation: The standard process states are: New, Ready, Running, Waiting (Blocked), and Terminated. 'Compiling' is not a process state recognized by the OS.

intermediate.question: The fork() system call in Unix/Linux:
options: Terminates the current process
Creates a new child process that is a copy of the parent
Loads a new program into memory
Switches to kernel mode only
intermediate.explanation: fork() creates a new process by duplicating the calling process. The child gets a copy of the parent's address space. fork() returns 0 in the child and the child's PID in the parent.

intermediate.question: Inter-process communication (IPC) can be achieved through:
options: Shared memory and message passing
Only hardware interrupts
Only signals
Only pipes
intermediate.explanation: IPC allows processes to communicate and synchronize. The two main approaches are shared memory (processes share a memory region) and message passing (processes exchange messages via the kernel).

intermediate.question: Context switching involves:
options: Saving the state of the current process and loading the state of the next process
Permanently terminating a process
Allocating more memory to a process
Compiling the process code
intermediate.explanation: A context switch saves the PCB of the running process and loads the PCB of the next scheduled process. This allows the CPU to switch between processes, enabling multitasking.

intermediate.question: Which scheduling algorithm allocates CPU to the process that arrives first?
options: Shortest Job First
First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)
Round Robin
Priority Scheduling
intermediate.explanation: FCFS allocates the CPU to processes in the order they arrive in the ready queue. It is simple but can cause the convoy effect where short processes wait behind long ones.

intermediate.question: Round Robin scheduling is primarily based on:
options: Process priority
Burst time
A fixed time quantum given to each process in cyclic order
Memory usage
intermediate.explanation: Round Robin assigns a fixed time slice (quantum) to each process. When the quantum expires, the process is preempted and placed at the end of the ready queue.

intermediate.question: Which scheduling algorithm can cause starvation of low-priority processes?
options: FCFS
Round Robin
Priority Scheduling (without aging)
Shortest Job First (non-preemptive)
intermediate.explanation: In priority scheduling, low-priority processes may wait indefinitely if higher-priority processes keep arriving. Aging (gradually increasing priority of waiting processes) solves this.

intermediate.question: Turnaround time is defined as:
options: The time a process spends waiting in the ready queue
The total time from submission of a process to its completion
The time between two consecutive context switches
The time the CPU is idle
intermediate.explanation: Turnaround time = Completion time - Arrival time. It includes waiting time, execution time, and I/O time.

intermediate.question: Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling is optimal in terms of:
options: Response time
Minimum average waiting time
Fairness
Throughput only
intermediate.explanation: SJF minimizes average waiting time by scheduling the process with the shortest burst time next. However, it can be difficult to predict burst times and may cause starvation.

intermediate.question: The preemptive version of SJF is known as:
options: FCFS
Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF)
Round Robin
Multilevel Queue
intermediate.explanation: SRTF is preemptive SJF: whenever a new process arrives with a shorter remaining burst time than the currently running process, the CPU is preempted and given to the new process.

intermediate.question: A thread is often called a:
options: Heavy-weight process
Lightweight process
Kernel module
Device driver
intermediate.explanation: Threads are called lightweight processes because they share the same address space and resources of their parent process, making context switching between threads faster than between processes.

intermediate.question: Threads within the same process share all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Code section
Data section (global variables)
Stack (each thread has its own)
Open file descriptors
intermediate.explanation: Each thread has its own stack, program counter, and register set. However, threads share the code, data, and heap sections, as well as open files and signal handlers.

intermediate.question: User-level threads are managed by:
options: The kernel scheduler
A thread library in user space
The hardware directly
The BIOS
intermediate.explanation: User-level threads are managed by a thread library (like POSIX Pthreads in user mode). The kernel is unaware of them, which means a blocking call by one thread can block the entire process.

intermediate.question: Which multithreading model maps many user-level threads to many kernel threads?
options: Many-to-One
One-to-One
Many-to-Many
One-to-Many
intermediate.explanation: The Many-to-Many model multiplexes many user threads onto an equal or smaller number of kernel threads, combining the advantages of both user-level and kernel-level threading.

intermediate.question: What is the key advantage of kernel-level threads over user-level threads?
options: They are faster to create
If one thread blocks on I/O, other threads in the same process can still run
They use less memory
They do not require OS support
intermediate.explanation: Kernel-level threads are managed by the OS. If one thread makes a blocking system call, the kernel can schedule another thread from the same process, unlike user-level threads where the entire process blocks.

intermediate.question: A race condition occurs when:
options: Two processes are scheduled on different CPUs
The outcome of execution depends on the unpredictable order of concurrent access to shared data
A process runs faster than expected
Memory is full
intermediate.explanation: A race condition arises when multiple processes/threads access shared data concurrently and the result depends on the specific timing of their execution.

intermediate.question: The critical section problem requires solutions to satisfy all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Mutual exclusion
Progress
Bounded waiting
Starvation guarantee for all processes at all times
intermediate.explanation: The three requirements are: Mutual Exclusion (only one process in the critical section), Progress (if no one is in the CS, a waiting process should enter), and Bounded Waiting (a limit on how long a process waits).

intermediate.question: A semaphore is:
options: A hardware device
A synchronization tool that uses an integer variable with atomic wait (P) and signal (V) operations
A type of CPU register
A scheduling algorithm
intermediate.explanation: A semaphore is an integer variable accessed through two atomic operations: wait() (decrement/block) and signal() (increment/wake). It is used for mutual exclusion and synchronization.

intermediate.question: A binary semaphore (mutex) can take values:
options: Any positive integer
0 and 1 only
Negative numbers only
-1 to 1
intermediate.explanation: A binary semaphore has only two states: 0 (locked/unavailable) and 1 (unlocked/available). It is used to enforce mutual exclusion for a single resource.

intermediate.question: Which classic synchronization problem involves producers adding items to a bounded buffer and consumers removing them?
options: Dining Philosophers
Readers-Writers
Producer-Consumer (Bounded Buffer)
Sleeping Barber
intermediate.explanation: The Producer-Consumer problem uses a shared buffer of fixed size. Producers add items when the buffer is not full, and consumers remove items when it is not empty, requiring synchronization.

intermediate.question: Which of the following are Coffman's four necessary conditions for deadlock?
options: Mutual exclusion, Hold and wait, No preemption, Circular wait
Starvation, Priority inversion, Aging, Preemption
Race condition, Deadlock, Livelock, Busy waiting
Scheduling, Paging, Segmentation, Swapping
intermediate.explanation: All four conditions must hold simultaneously for deadlock: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait.

intermediate.question: The Banker's Algorithm is used for:
options: CPU scheduling
Deadlock avoidance by checking for safe states before allocating resources
Memory allocation
Disk scheduling
intermediate.explanation: The Banker's Algorithm checks whether granting a resource request would leave the system in a safe state (where all processes can complete). If not, the request is denied to avoid deadlock.

intermediate.question: Deadlock prevention works by:
options: Detecting deadlocks after they occur and recovering
Ensuring that at least one of the four necessary conditions cannot hold
Ignoring deadlocks entirely
Using only FCFS scheduling
intermediate.explanation: Deadlock prevention eliminates the possibility of deadlock by structurally ensuring at least one of Coffman's four conditions can never be satisfied.

intermediate.question: Deadlock detection in a system with multiple resource instances typically uses:
options: Banker's Algorithm
A wait-for graph or resource allocation graph
Round Robin scheduling
Paging
intermediate.explanation: Deadlock detection algorithms use resource allocation graphs. For single instances, a cycle in the wait-for graph indicates deadlock. For multiple instances, a matrix-based algorithm similar to Banker's is used.

intermediate.question: Which is NOT a valid deadlock recovery method?
options: Process termination (abort one or more processes)
Resource preemption (take resources from a process)
Increasing CPU clock speed
Rolling back a process to a safe checkpoint
intermediate.explanation: Deadlock recovery involves terminating processes, preempting resources, or rolling back processes. Increasing CPU speed does nothing to break the circular wait.

intermediate.question: Paging eliminates which type of memory fragmentation?
options: Internal fragmentation
External fragmentation
Both internal and external
Neither
intermediate.explanation: Paging divides memory into fixed-size frames and processes into same-size pages. Since any free frame can be allocated, external fragmentation is eliminated. However, internal fragmentation can still occur in the last page.

intermediate.question: A page table maps:
options: Physical addresses to disk locations
Virtual (logical) page numbers to physical frame numbers
Process IDs to memory sizes
File names to inode numbers
intermediate.explanation: The page table translates virtual addresses used by a process to physical addresses in RAM. Each entry maps a page number to its corresponding frame number.

intermediate.question: Segmentation divides a process's address space based on:
options: Fixed-size blocks
Logical divisions such as code, data, stack, and heap
Random partitions
Equal-size pages
intermediate.explanation: Segmentation divides memory according to the logical structure of a program (code segment, data segment, stack segment). Segments can vary in size, unlike fixed-size pages.

intermediate.question: The first-fit, best-fit, and worst-fit are strategies for:
options: CPU scheduling
Contiguous memory allocation from free memory holes
Disk scheduling
Page replacement
intermediate.explanation: These are strategies for choosing which free memory block to allocate: First-fit picks the first large enough hole, Best-fit picks the smallest sufficient hole, and Worst-fit picks the largest hole.

intermediate.question: A page fault occurs when:
options: The CPU overheats
A process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory, requiring it to be loaded from disk
The page table is corrupted
Two processes share the same page
intermediate.explanation: A page fault is an interrupt triggered when a process references a page not present in RAM. The OS then loads the required page from disk (swap space) into a free frame, updates the page table, and resumes the process.

intermediate.question: An inode in Unix/Linux file systems stores:
options: Only the file name
File metadata (permissions, size, timestamps, pointers to data blocks) but NOT the file name
Only the file content
The process ID of the file creator
intermediate.explanation: An inode stores file metadata: owner, permissions, size, timestamps, and pointers to data blocks on disk. The file name is stored in the directory entry, not the inode.

intermediate.question: Which file allocation method suffers from external fragmentation?
options: Contiguous allocation
Linked allocation
Indexed allocation
Both linked and indexed
intermediate.explanation: Contiguous allocation requires each file to occupy consecutive disk blocks, leading to external fragmentation as files are created and deleted. Linked and indexed allocation avoid this.

intermediate.question: In a journaling file system, the journal is used to:
options: Store user passwords
Record metadata changes before committing them, enabling crash recovery
Compress files
Schedule disk I/O
intermediate.explanation: A journal logs intended file system changes before they are applied. If a crash occurs, the journal can be replayed to restore the file system to a consistent state.

intermediate.question: The absolute path name of a file:
options: Is relative to the current working directory
Begins at the root directory and follows the path down to the file
Contains only the file name
Is stored inside the file itself
intermediate.explanation: An absolute path starts from the root directory (e.g., /home/safwan/file.txt). A relative path starts from the current working directory.

intermediate.question: Which file allocation method uses an index block to store pointers to all data blocks of a file?
options: Contiguous allocation
Linked allocation
Indexed allocation
Sequential allocation
intermediate.explanation: Indexed allocation keeps all pointers to a file's data blocks in a single index block. This allows efficient direct access without external fragmentation, though it uses extra space for the index.

intermediate.question: Which disk scheduling algorithm services requests in the order they arrive?
options: SCAN
SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)
FCFS
C-SCAN
intermediate.explanation: FCFS disk scheduling processes requests in arrival order. It is fair but can result in large head movements if requests are scattered across the disk.

intermediate.question: RAID stands for:
options: Random Access Integrated Disk
Redundant Array of Independent (or Inexpensive) Disks
Reliable Automated Input Device
Recursive Algorithm for Indexed Data
intermediate.explanation: RAID combines multiple physical disks into a logical unit for improved performance, reliability, or both, using techniques like striping, mirroring, and parity.

intermediate.question: Swap space is used by the OS to:
options: Store device drivers
Extend physical memory by storing pages/processes that are swapped out of RAM onto disk
Boot the operating system
Store the file system journal
intermediate.explanation: Swap space on disk acts as an extension of RAM. When physical memory is full, the OS moves inactive pages to swap space, freeing RAM for active processes.

intermediate.question: DMA (Direct Memory Access) allows:
options: The CPU to directly access disk sectors
I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention for each byte
Memory to be accessed without an OS
Processes to bypass the file system
intermediate.explanation: DMA offloads data transfer from the CPU. The DMA controller handles the transfer between I/O devices and memory, interrupting the CPU only when the transfer is complete.

intermediate.question: Spooling is a technique where:
options: Data is compressed before storage
I/O jobs (e.g., print jobs) are queued on disk to handle access to slow sequential devices
The CPU clock speed is adjusted
Memory is partitioned
intermediate.explanation: Spooling (Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line) queues I/O requests on disk so that slow devices like printers can process them independently while the CPU continues other work.

intermediate.question: A device driver is:
options: A physical component of the I/O device
Software that provides an interface between the OS kernel and a specific hardware device
A CPU scheduling algorithm
A memory management unit
intermediate.explanation: Device drivers translate OS commands into device-specific operations. They act as intermediaries between the OS and hardware, abstracting device details from the kernel.

intermediate.question: Buffering in I/O systems is used to:
options: Permanently store data
Temporarily hold data during transfer between devices with different speeds
Encrypt data in transit
Replace the cache
intermediate.explanation: Buffers are temporary memory areas that smooth out speed mismatches between producers and consumers of data, such as between a fast CPU and a slow I/O device.

intermediate.question: Polling (busy waiting) for I/O completion means the CPU:
options: Sleeps until an interrupt arrives
Repeatedly checks the device status register until the operation is done
Delegates all I/O to the DMA controller
Ignores the I/O request
intermediate.explanation: In polling, the CPU continuously checks (in a loop) whether the I/O device has completed its operation. This wastes CPU cycles compared to interrupt-driven I/O, where the device signals completion via an interrupt.

intermediate.question: The principle of least privilege states that:
options: All users should have administrator access
A process or user should be given only the minimum access rights needed to perform its task
Security is not needed in single-user systems
All files should be publicly readable
